ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for celtuce (whole); the cooked dish form (blanched or stir-fried) is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lettuces and other salad plants — cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked lettuces and salad plants; celtuce/wosun is not individually coded. Adapted match used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for asparagus lettuce / celtuce as a food item.

main_ingredients: light soy sauce or oyster sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of stir-fried or blanched asparagus lettuce is approximately 150 g, as typically served at a Singaporean hawker or casual dining stall.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Celtuce, raw (FDC ID 169247) adapted for cooked preparation with light seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw celtuce (FDC ID 169247) used as the primary nutritional anchor. Values adjusted upward for sodium (300 mg/100g) to reflect light soy sauce or oyster sauce seasoning typical of this preparation, and for fat (3.33 g/100g) to reflect stir-frying oil. Energy, protein, carbohydrate, fibre, calcium, iron, and vitamin C are consistent with the USDA raw celtuce baseline adjusted for cooking losses. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but does not contain a specific entry for cooked wosun/celtuce; USDA adapted match is the closest approved-source equivalent.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for asparagus lettuce (wosun/celtuce). GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy/stem vegetables (e.g., lettuce GI ~15, green vegetables generally <20) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.0) / 100 = 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 300 mg per 100g from soy sauce or oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce when ordering this dish, and prefer blanching over stir-frying to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per 100g; the vegetable's natural potassium content provides partial offset per DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.0 g/100g), low sugar (1.33 g/100g), and 2.0 g/100g dietary fibre supporting low glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has an estimated GI of 15 and a glycaemic load of 0.9 per 150g serving, making it an excellent low-GI vegetable side for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; patients should limit added sauces to control sugar and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.53 g/100g), zero trans fat, and 2.0 g/100g dietary fibre supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and meaningful dietary fibre content make this dish a heart-healthy vegetable side; patients with cardiovascular disease should opt for blanching and minimal oil to further reduce fat intake, and request reduced soy sauce to manage sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant-containing vegetable with vitamin C (10 mg/100g) and negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Asparagus lettuce provides vitamin C and fibre with negligible saturated fat, supporting an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise soy sauce and oyster sauce to keep sodium below 400 mg/100g and prefer blanching to preserve heat-sensitive antioxidants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried or Blanched Asparagus Lettuce (莴笋, wosun, celtuce) is a simple Singaporean Chinese vegetable side dish featuring the crisp, pale-green stem of the celtuce plant (Lactuca sativa var. angustana), which is botanically a stem lettuce rather than a true asparagus. The vegetable is either briefly blanched in boiling water or stir-fried in a hot wok with garlic and a light seasoning of soy sauce or oyster sauce, preserving its characteristic mild, slightly bitter flavour and satisfying crunchy texture. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to braised noodle dishes, rice plates, or as part of a multi-dish hawker meal. Nutritionally, this dish is low in calories (approximately 53 kcal per 100g), low in carbohydrates (4.0 g/100g), and provides 2.0 g/100g dietary fibre, 2.33 g/100g protein, and 10 mg/100g vitamin C. The sodium content of approximately 300 mg/100g reflects light sauce seasoning. The estimated glycaemic index is 15 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is appropriate for vegetarians and is halal-friendly. Clinically, it is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or weight, and is acceptable for hypertension patients when sauce quantities are moderated.
common_user_queries: Is asparagus lettuce good for diabetes?
Low carb vegetable side dish Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact entry for cooked asparagus lettuce (wosun/celtuce) exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw celtuce (FDC ID 169247) with adjustments for cooking method and light sauce seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from non-starchy leafy/stem vegetable data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked celtuce/wosun is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169247) or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific cooked preparation with sauce is not reported in the primary adapted source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific cooked dish preparation in any approved source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are present in this plant-based vegetable dish prepared with vegetable oil. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived fat; cholesterol is naturally zero. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not contain a specific nutrient entry for cooked wosun/celtuce; USDA FoodData Central adapted match is used as the closest approved-source equivalent. The original input data_source was listed as 'grabfood' which is a forbidden source for primary nutrient evidence; values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central.
